APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper addresses a core NLP task (sentiment classification) and proposes linguistically motivated regularizers integrated with LSTM/Bi-LSTM, which is directly relevant to top-tier NLP/ML audiences. It evaluates on standard benchmarks (MR, SST) and compares to strong baselines (LSTM, Bi-LSTM, Tree-LSTM, CNN variants), fitting community expectations for empirical work. From a reproducibility-engineer standpoint I note missing hyperparameter reporting and no code mention, which are minor scope concerns for acceptance but do not undermine topical fit. Overall the submission is a good fit for a major NLP/ML venue, though improved experimental reporting would strengthen it. | B(4): This paper is well within the scope of a top-tier NLP venue: it addresses sentiment composition (negation/intensification) with linguistically motivated regularizers and evaluates on standard benchmarks (MR, SST) against appropriate baselines (LSTM/Bi-LSTM, Tree-LSTM, CNN variants). The formulation and evaluation (accuracy, targeted subset analyses) match community expectations, and the work targets the right audience interested in linguistically informed neural models. My only reservation is the limited practical impact of NR/IR noted by the authors (small fraction of examples with negators/intensifiers) and the omission of explicit negation scope modeling, which are minor scope concerns rather than venue misalignment.

From a reproducibility-engineer standpoint I note missing hyperparameter reporting and no code mention, which are minor scope concerns for acceptance but do not undermine topical fit. Overall the submission is a good fit for a major NLP/ML venue, though improved experimental reporting would strengthen it.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper is well organized with a logical flow from introduction through methods and experiments, and the main contributions (LR-LSTM/LR-Bi-LSTM and four linguistic regularizers) are stated early. Notation and formulation appear consistent and the use of KL-based regularizers is described clearly; figures/tables and examples are informative for illustrating linguistic effects. Writing is generally good and readable, though a few simplifications and stated limitations (e.g., ignoring negation scope) introduce some ambiguity about applicability that could have been better signposted. Overall presentation is strong with only minor clarity issues. | B(4): The paper is generally well organized with a clear flow from motivation through method to experiments and conclusion, and the main contributions (LR-LSTM/LR-Bi-LSTM and four linguistic regularizers) are stated early. Notation appears consistent and the use of KL-divergence margin losses and parameterizations (shifting distributions, transformation matrices) is explained, though hyperparameter details are missing which would aid reproducibility. Figures, tables, and examples are reported as effective and informative, and related work coverage is thorough; minor clarity could be improved by explicitly reporting hyperparameters and code availability. Overall the writing is good with few ambiguities, making the paper readable and useful to linguistically-minded NLP readers.

IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): This work addresses a relevant problem—modeling linguistic phenomena (negation, intensity) in sentiment classification—and proposes a practical mechanism (four linguistic regularizers applied to LSTM/Bi-LSTM) that yields targeted improvements, particularly on negator/intensifier subsets. The contribution is useful and likely to inspire follow-up work integrating explicit linguistic constraints into sequence models, but it is incremental: gains are constrained by dataset statistics (only 14%/23% of test sentences contain negation/intensity) and the paper concedes important limitations (no negation scope resolution), which limit broad transformative impact. Overall, the paper offers a moderate-impact advance that could be built upon but is unlikely to fundamentally change the field by itself. | B(3): The paper tackles an important practical challenge—modeling sentiment composition under negation and intensity—by introducing four linguistically motivated regularizers applied to LSTM/Bi-LSTM and demonstrating targeted gains (notably on negator/intensifier subsets). While this approach yields useful interpretability (shift distributions and transformation matrices) and can influence how researchers incorporate linguistic constraints into sequence models, the impact is moderated by simplifications (no negation scope resolution, sequence-only encoding) and limited prevalence of negation/intensity cases in the evaluated datasets, so it is unlikely to be transformative but is a solid, moderately influential contribution.

The contribution is useful and likely to inspire follow-up work integrating explicit linguistic constraints into sequence models, but it is incremental: gains are constrained by dataset statistics (only 14%/23% of test sentences contain negation/intensity) and the paper concedes important limitations (no negation scope resolution), which limit broad transformative impact. Overall, the paper offers a moderate-impact advance that could be built upon but is unlikely to fundamentally change the field by itself.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper compares against a broad set of appropriate baselines (RNN/RNTN, LSTM/Bi-LSTM, Tree-LSTM, CNN variants, DAN, NCSL) on standard datasets (MR, SST) using the same accuracy metric, and performs targeted analyses (negator/intensifier subsets) that clarify where gains arise. However, reproducibility details are missing (no code or hyperparameter reporting noted), which raises fairness concerns about tuning and experimental conditions; it is also unclear whether multiple runs/variance or full ablations were reported to isolate each regularizer’s effect. The authors do acknowledge limitations and dataset coverage for negation/intensity and discuss related work thoroughly, so comparisons are good but not fully comprehensive for reproducibility and ablation rigor. | B(4): The paper compares against an appropriate and relevant set of baselines (RNN/RNTN, LSTM/Bi-LSTM, Tree-LSTM, CNN variants, DAN, NCSL) on standard datasets (MR, SST) using the same accuracy metric, which supports fair head-to-head evaluation. It also includes targeted analyses (negator/intensifier subsets) that help expose where the regularizers matter. However, the summary does not make it clear that the authors explicitly acknowledge cases where prior methods perform comparably or worse, nor does it detail hyperparameter/code parity to fully rule out implementation or tuning differences—hence a small deduction for incomplete comparative transparency.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper compares against a broad set of appropriate baselines (RNN/RNTN, LSTM/Bi-LSTM, Tree-LSTM, CNN variants, DAN, NCSL) on standard datasets (MR, SST) using the same accuracy metric, and performs targeted analyses (negator/intensifier subsets) that clarify where gains arise. However, reproducibility details are missing (no code or hyperparameter reporting noted), which raises fairness concerns about tuning and experimental conditions; it is also unclear whether multiple runs/variance or full ablations were reported to isolate each regularizer’s effect. The authors do acknowledge limitations and dataset coverage for negation/intensity and discuss related work thoroughly, so comparisons are good but not fully comprehensive for reproducibility and ablation rigor.

ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper proposes a concrete novel mechanism—four linguistically motivated regularizers (NSR, SR, NR, IR) implemented as class-shift distributions and word-specific transformation matrices enforced via KL-margin losses—applied to LSTM/Bi-LSTM for sentiment classification. This is a non-trivial and well-differentiated extension beyond vanilla LSTM models and prior lexicon-aware approaches, especially in how it parameterizes negation/intensity effects. However, the underlying problem (sentiment classification) is well-studied and the contribution reads as an incremental methodological innovation rather than a radical new paradigm, so it should be judged as moderate novelty. | B(3): The paper proposes a clear, linguistically motivated set of regularizers (NSR, SR, NR, IR) applied to LSTM/Bi-LSTM to enforce sentiment shifts, which is a concrete and interpretable extension beyond vanilla sequence models. However, modelling negation/intensity as learned transformation matrices and using KL-margin constraints over adjacent positions is an incremental synthesis of existing ideas (linguistically informed priors, sentiment-shift modeling, matrix-based word transforms) rather than a radical new paradigm. The authors do appear to differentiate their contributions from prior work and provide targeted evaluation on negation/intensifier subsets, so the work would inform experts but is not highly novel.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper proposes a concrete novel mechanism—four linguistically motivated regularizers (NSR, SR, NR, IR) implemented as class-shift distributions and word-specific transformation matrices enforced via KL-margin losses—applied to LSTM/Bi-LSTM for sentiment classification. This is a non-trivial and well-differentiated extension beyond vanilla LSTM models and prior lexicon-aware approaches, especially in how it parameterizes negation/intensity effects. However, the underlying problem (sentiment classification) is well-studied and the contribution reads as an incremental methodological innovation rather than a radical new paradigm, so it should be judged as moderate novelty.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper proposes a clear, linguistically motivated set of regularizers (NSR, SR, NR, IR) applied to LSTM/Bi-LSTM to enforce sentiment shifts, which is a concrete and interpretable extension beyond vanilla sequence models. However, modelling negation/intensity as learned transformation matrices and using KL-margin constraints over adjacent positions is an incremental synthesis of existing ideas (linguistically informed priors, sentiment-shift modeling, matrix-based word transforms) rather than a radical new paradigm. The authors do appear to differentiate their contributions from prior work and provide targeted evaluation on negation/intensifier subsets, so the work would inform experts but is not highly novel.

SUBSTANCE.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The experiments provide useful comparisons to strong baselines (LSTM/Bi-LSTM, Tree-LSTM, CNN variants) and include targeted subset analysis showing gains on negator/intensifier cases, and the paper explicitly discusses limitations (scope of negation, limited frequency of such words). However, key reproducibility details are missing (no hyperparameters reported, code not mentioned, no information about multiple runs or variance), and the summary does not mention explicit ablation or sensitivity studies isolating each regularizer’s contribution. Related work appears thorough, but the lack of ablations and experimental detail prevents full confidence that the empirical evidence convincingly supports the claims. | B(3): The experiments compare LR-LSTM/LR-Bi-LSTM to a broad set of baselines (LSTM/Bi-LSTM, Tree-LSTM, CNN variants, NCSL) on MR and SST and report gains especially on negation/intensifier subsets, which supports the core claims. However the work appears to lack reported hyperparameters or released code, and there is no mention of thorough ablation or sensitivity analyses for the four regularizers (beyond targeted subset results), which weakens reproducibility and attribution of gains. Related work is described as thorough and the paper discusses limitations (negation scope, dataset coverage), but the experimental analysis is adequate rather than comprehensive.

reviewer_a.justification: The experiments provide useful comparisons to strong baselines (LSTM/Bi-LSTM, Tree-LSTM, CNN variants) and include targeted subset analysis showing gains on negator/intensifier cases, and the paper explicitly discusses limitations (scope of negation, limited frequency of such words). However, key reproducibility details are missing (no hyperparameters reported, code not mentioned, no information about multiple runs or variance), and the summary does not mention explicit ablation or sensitivity studies isolating each regularizer’s contribution. Related work appears thorough, but the lack of ablations and experimental detail prevents full confidence that the empirical evidence convincingly supports the claims.

reviewer_b.justification: The experiments compare LR-LSTM/LR-Bi-LSTM to a broad set of baselines (LSTM/Bi-LSTM, Tree-LSTM, CNN variants, NCSL) on MR and SST and report gains especially on negation/intensifier subsets, which supports the core claims. However the work appears to lack reported hyperparameters or released code, and there is no mention of thorough ablation or sensitivity analyses for the four regularizers (beyond targeted subset results), which weakens reproducibility and attribution of gains. Related work is described as thorough and the paper discusses limitations (negation scope, dataset coverage), but the experimental analysis is adequate rather than comprehensive.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper clearly states its main claims and provides experimental comparisons showing LR-Bi-LSTM improvements, particularly on negator/intensifier subsets, so there is empirical support for the core idea. However, important reproducibility details are missing (no reported hyperparameters or code, no mention of multiple runs or statistical significance), and evaluation relies only on accuracy without sufficient variance reporting, which weakens confidence in the results. The mathematical formulation of the regularizers appears plausible from the summary, but the paper makes unsupported simplifying assumptions (e.g., ignoring negation scope and relying on sequence LSTMs for syntactic effects) that introduce potential confounds. Overall the contribution is promising but the experimental methodology and reporting need strengthening to fully justify the conclusions. | B(3): The paper's main claims are generally supported by experiments showing LR-Bi-LSTM improvements over standard LSTM/Bi-LSTM and gains on negator/intensifier subsets, but key experimental details (hyperparameters, statistical significance, code) are missing which weakens reproducibility. The mathematical apparatus—KL-margin regularizers and parameterized shift/transform matrices—appears plausible and internally consistent from the summary, with no glaring derivation errors reported. Methodologically the baselines are appropriate, but the paper admits (and I concur) that ignoring negation scope and relying on sequence LSTMs for syntactic/semantic effects is a substantive simplifying assumption; coupled with low prevalence of negators/intensifiers in the data, this limits the strength of claims about modeling negation/intensity. Overall the conclusions are partially justified by the reported results but would need more rigorous experimental controls and analysis to fully validate the linguistic claims.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper clearly states its main claims and provides experimental comparisons showing LR-Bi-LSTM improvements, particularly on negator/intensifier subsets, so there is empirical support for the core idea. However, important reproducibility details are missing (no reported hyperparameters or code, no mention of multiple runs or statistical significance), and evaluation relies only on accuracy without sufficient variance reporting, which weakens confidence in the results. The mathematical formulation of the regularizers appears plausible from the summary, but the paper makes unsupported simplifying assumptions (e.g., ignoring negation scope and relying on sequence LSTMs for syntactic effects) that introduce potential confounds. Overall the contribution is promising but the experimental methodology and reporting need strengthening to fully justify the conclusions.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper's main claims are generally supported by experiments showing LR-Bi-LSTM improvements over standard LSTM/Bi-LSTM and gains on negator/intensifier subsets, but key experimental details (hyperparameters, statistical significance, code) are missing which weakens reproducibility. The mathematical apparatus—KL-margin regularizers and parameterized shift/transform matrices—appears plausible and internally consistent from the summary, with no glaring derivation errors reported. Methodologically the baselines are appropriate, but the paper admits (and I concur) that ignoring negation scope and relying on sequence LSTMs for syntactic/semantic effects is a substantive simplifying assumption; coupled with low prevalence of negators/intensifiers in the data, this limits the strength of claims about modeling negation/intensity. Overall the conclusions are partially justified by the reported results but would need more rigorous experimental controls and analysis to fully validate the linguistic claims.

main_claims: Proposes linguistically regularized LSTM models (LR-LSTM and LR-Bi-LSTM) that incorporate linguistic regularizers for sentiment classification.

Introduces four linguistic regularizers—Non-Sentiment Regularizer (NSR), Sentiment Regularizer (SR), Negation Regularizer (NR), and Intensity Regularizer (IR)—parameterized by shifting distributions or transformation matrices to model sentiment drift, negation, and intensity effects.

Shows that the proposed models can capture the linguistic roles of sentiment words, negation words, and intensity words, and that LR-Bi-LSTM outperforms baselines (notably Bi-LSTM) on targeted subsets containing negators or intensifiers.
_summary.methodology: Apply linguistic regularizers (NSR, SR, NR, IR) to LSTM and bidirectional LSTM by constraining predicted sentiment distributions between adjacent positions using KL-divergence margin losses; model sentiment shifts with class-specific shifting distributions and negation/intensity effects with word-specific transformation matrices.
limitations: Do not consider the modification span (scope) of negation and intensity words; negation scope resolution is beyond the scope of this work.
Sentiment shifting/negation/intensity effects depend on syntax and semantics, but the paper resorts to sequence LSTM for encoding surrounding contexts (simplification).
Only 14% of sentences contain negation words and 23% contain intensity words in the test datasets, limiting impact of NR and IR.

reviewer_a.name: Methodical Reproducibility Engineer
reviewer_a.background: Senior ML engineer with a PhD in NLP focusing on reproducible experimentation and model analysis; extensive experience implementing LSTM-based models and running rigorous empirical comparisons.
traits: expects clear descriptions of model architectures, training hyperparameters, and optimization details sufficient to reproduce results
looks for strong baselines, properly tuned comparisons, and ablation studies that isolate the contribution of each component
values appropriate statistical testing, multiple runs with reporting of variance, and analysis on both aggregate and targeted subsets
notices implementation design choices that can confound results (e.g., pretraining, embedding handling, early stopping, data preprocessing) and requests clarification
reviewer_a.reviewing_style: Focuses on experimental soundness and reproducibility, verifying whether the empirical evidence supports the claims and suggesting additional experiments or reporting needed to make the study convincing.

reviewer_b.name: NLP Semantics & Impact Researcher
reviewer_b.background: Associate professor in NLP with a track record on sentiment analysis, compositional semantics, and linguistically informed neural models; interested in how linguistic insights translate into model innovations and broader utility.
traits: evaluates novelty relative to prior linguistically motivated models and whether proposed regularizers advance understanding of compositional sentiment
assesses the scope and significance of results: when the method matters (e.g., negation/intensification cases) and potential practical impact

values clear framing in relation to related work (e.g., sentiment shifting, syntactic/semantic compositionality, prior negation/intensity modeling) and theoretical or qualitative analysis of learned components
looks for interpretability and whether learned parameters (shifts, transformation matrices) align with linguistic expectations
reviewer_b.reviewing_style: Judges the paper by how much new linguistic insight or practical benefit it brings to sentiment modeling, and whether the work is sufficiently novel and well-situated in the literature to merit publication.
